title: Publish deterministic JSON test vectors for cross-implementation conformance
description: Create a `spec/test-vectors/` directory with 30+ JSON files covering: valid handshake round-trip, expired agent credential rejection, stale Merkle root, scope subset violation, cumulative bit encoding violations (bit 4 without 3, bit 3 without 2), delegation chain at max depth, and nullifier replay. Each vector includes deterministic inputs (using a fixed secret/key pair), expected intermediate values (commitment hashes), and expected pass/fail outcome. Reference these from the IETF draft §A (Appendix). This is the single most impactful interop artifact — any alternative SDK implementation can validate correctness against these vectors.

title: Specify human tree root history buffer matching agent tree parity
description: IdentityRegistry maintains a 30-root history buffer for agentTree but has no equivalent for humanTree — the contract comment says 'humanTree uses Semaphore's' but the code uses a standalone LeanIMT with no history. This means any new human enrollment instantly invalidates all in-flight human proofs. Add a `humanRootHistory[30]` ring buffer with the same `isKnownHumanRoot()` check pattern used by `isKnownAgentRoot()`. Document the staleness window (30 enrollments ≈ N minutes at expected throughput) in the IETF draft Security Considerations §7.

title: Chain-scoped nullifier domain separation
description: The current nullifierHash in HumanUniqueness is Poseidon2(scope, secret) and in AgentPolicy is Poseidon2(credentialCommitment, sessionNonce). Neither includes a chain identifier, so a valid proof on Base can be replayed on Arbitrum or Polygon if the same IdentityRegistry is deployed with the same roots. Add a public input chainId to all three circuits and bind it into the nullifier: Poseidon3(scope, secret, chainId) for humans, Poseidon3(credentialCommitment, sessionNonce, chainId) for agents. The on-chain verifier checks chainId == block.chainid.

title: Standardize proof envelope wire format with IANA content type
description: The SDK currently passes proof objects as ad-hoc JSON blobs with no schema versioning or content negotiation. Define a versioned proof envelope (`application/bolyra-proof+cbor` / `+json`) with fields: `version`, `circuitId`, `provingSystem` (groth16|plonk), `publicSignals[]`, `proof` (π_A, π_B, π_C or equivalent), and `metadata` (chain, registryAddress). Add an ABNF grammar to the IETF draft §4. This is prerequisite for any multi-implementation interop — without it, each SDK reinvents serialization.

title: Complete DID resolution algorithm with verification methods
description: The existing `spec/did-method-bolyra.md` defines the DID syntax but lacks a complete resolution algorithm per W3C DID Core §7.1. Specify: (1) how `did:bolyra:<commitment>` resolves to a DID Document by querying IdentityRegistry events, (2) verification method entries mapping to the EdDSA Baby Jubjub keys (for agents) or the nullifier-based authentication (for humans), (3) service endpoints pointing to the on-chain registry. Without this, no DID resolver library can implement `did:bolyra` support.

title: Off-chain session token (JWT) after on-chain handshake
description: After a successful on-chain handshake verification, emit a signed JWT session token that encodes the verified scope, nullifiers, and expiry. Subsequent API calls within the session present this JWT instead of re-proving on-chain. This is the critical path to production adoption: no agentic commerce platform will pay gas per API call. Implement a SessionTokenIssuer in the SDK that takes handshake proof outputs and returns a compact JWT, plus a verifySessionToken() function that checks signature, expiry, and scope without touching the chain. Add a spec section defining the JWT claims (scope_commitment, human_nullifier, agent_nullifier, session_nonce, iat, exp).

title: Define off-chain session token format for verified handshakes
description: After a successful on-chain handshake verification, there is no standardized bearer token format for subsequent off-chain API calls within the same session. Define a compact JWT-like session token (JWS over the handshake public signals: humanNullifier, agentNullifier, sessionNonce, scopeCommitment) with an IANA-registerable media type `application/bolyra-session+jwt`. This lets relying parties skip on-chain re-verification for the session lifetime while maintaining cryptographic binding to the original ZKP handshake.

title: Prove BinaryMerkleRoot depth parameter cannot be manipulated to accept invalid proofs
description: The `merkleProofLength` input in all three circuits controls how many siblings are actually used by BinaryMerkleRoot, but there is no constraint that `merkleProofLength` matches the expected tree depth. An attacker could supply a shorter proof path against a subtree root that happens to be in the root history buffer. Write a formal property (Certora-style invariant or circom assert) proving that for any accepted witness, the Merkle root output corresponds to a tree of exactly the deployed depth, or add an equality constraint `merkleProofLength === EXPECTED_DEPTH` to each circuit.
